speaker: interviewer
text: August 1, 1996. The survivor is Mr. Joseph Greenblatt. Interviewer, Miriam Rabner. We're in New York City, United States of America, and this interview will be conducted in English. I'm Miriam Rabner, R-A-B-N-E-R. Today's August 1, 1996. I'm in New York City, United States of America. And today, I'm interviewing Mr. Joseph Greenblatt. Please give me your full name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Greenblatt, J-O-S-E-P-H G-R-E-E-N-B-L-A-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born, Mr. Greenblatt?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 5, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born, who lived in your family? Tell me a little bit first about your father, then a little bit about your mother, and then about your siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Samuel or Shmuel Israel Greenblatt. He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an importer of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother was Yena born Greenblatt. Same name. They were first cousins. She was a housewife. We were two children which were older than I. My sister was the oldest one. She was born in 1908. And my brother Siegmund was born in 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the children born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your parents from Warsaw originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, no. My father was born in Bogoria. That this in the District of Kielce. And my mother was born in Klimontow, District of Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both Polish citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were Polish citizen. By birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your father an orthodox man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in full orthodox sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: More traditional?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a-- a liberal. Was a national Jew. His religion came rather through nationalism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean? What did it mean at-- at his time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was involved later-- in the later years in-- very much in Zionism. Through partly my influence, because I was-- by being-- growing up in a non-Jewish atmosphere-- meaning my neighbors, we were only Jewish family within four blocks-- and I became involved in the Zionist Youth movements, and I influenced my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have contact with Jews and non-Jews in business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He spoke Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spoke Polish, spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak at home to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between them, they spoke a lot of Yiddish. With-- with us, they spoke Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get a newspaper in-- in either one of the languages, or in both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were subscriber to a Polish Jewish paper called [POLISH]. Was-- they used to call it a Jewish organ in the Polish language. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's what we were reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the later years, till the radio was discovered. It-- it took a while, was already [INAUDIBLE]. It was already of the crystal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Had ear phones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father's parents move to be near you, or did you go and visit them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was by train about-- it was a total about eight hours by train, and then we had to go by horse and buggy to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that a highlight when you went to visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I loved it. I was going there every summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the whole summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I split it between one grandmother and the other grandmother. As my grandmother later on, my father's mother, moved from Bogoria to [POLISH], to her oldest son, and I was going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were either of your grandparents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, both of them. Rather orthodox. They were living in Shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were exposed to that kind of life during your summer vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And I liked it, in a way. Although I'm not orthodox, but I envied them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I-- I'll tell you. To be orthodox, you deprive yourself of many things in life. And I was always saying, I'm not orthodox because it's easier. But really, I admire orthodoxy. Of course, not certain orthodoxy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Modern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Modern orthodoxy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever go to a synagogue or to a temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: We belonged to the-- this Great Synagogue of Warsaw. That is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. That was the whole fun, to be with other kids sent to be-- and sent to be outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the high holidays, yes. We were all going.

speaker: interviewer
text: The women went also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: When the women separated from the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. See, as they call it, the Great Synagogue of Warsaw was a conservative synagogue, but it wasn't. Women were separated. Women were sitting upstairs. Men downstairs. And the choir was sitting upstairs. Only thing, there was [HEBREW], the separation between the [INAUDIBLE] and the-- and this. And the people in the canto was facing the audience.

speaker: interviewer
text: You always know you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Oh, they let me know. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my environment. Day IN school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went to strictly non-Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A private non-Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: A private, non-Jewish school. [INAUDIBLE], the name of the school was. That was the-- the founder of the school. I was the only Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first and the last.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you got admitted to the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to go back to my father's background. My father was a Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] freedom fighter in the Pilsudski's legion. And there for-- and the so-called [SPEAKING POLISH], the first charter, which originated in Krakow. And he, with Pilsudski, and whenever [POLISH], with all the generals, was going fighting through the Russians to lead them to Warsaw with Pilsudski. And was very close with all of them, and remained to the last days of Poland's Polish existence. And he used his influence. It was for him a point of honor that his son has to be admitted to one of the best schools in-- in Warsaw. If not in a government school, so at least [POLISH]. And that's where I-- and I remember that, it was starting from the high echelon, the director of the school was anti-Semite. Through the teachers and the enrolled children, they were anti-Semites-- anti-Semites. And they let me know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what would they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name callings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? What would they call? [POLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH]. In a derogative way, they were addressing me sometimes till I got older and I started to resist them. Then I was looking for an outlet. I wanted to join with them and the Boy Scouts. Didn't want to accept me because I'm Jewish. It was the Polish Boy Scouts, it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was one like [POLISH]. And Poland, and Warsaw especially, was a tradition that unlike [POLISH], the Jewish kids which were organizing in all kind of Zionist Youth movements, were going on excursions out of Warsaw with the steamship board. And in the evening when they returned, they had to parade through the Jewish neighborhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was going from my Polish neighborhood to watch it. And somehow, that [INAUDIBLE] woke up in me. That will be an outlet for me. They have their Boy Scouts. I'll have my Hashomer or whatever it is. And I joined the Hashomer Hatzair. It was not for a long time. I left Hashomer Hatzair. It was too left for me. And I joined a Hashomer [HEBREW]. And they were little bit more the right. It was not enough for me. And I split-- was one of those who split the Hashomer [HEBREW]. And we formed the Hasha [HEBREW], which later become the [HEBREW], or as now it's known, the Betar. The crypt of the Irgun [HEBREW]. And I felt very comfortable in it. And I grew to very high positions in that movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is-- I am still not-- not the Hayim Nahman Bialik. I am not a scholar in Hebrew. But while I became a leader in Betar, I wanted to learn Hebrew. My only Hebrew was that every day when I was a-- a rich 12 year-- my 12th birthday, my parents hired a teacher, a Hebrew teacher, for my Hebrew gym-- private gymnasium, from a scholar, who was coming and preparing me for my bar mitzvah. And it took a-- a year's time to put everything with a shovel into my head. And I of course didn't understand what I was saying, but had a beautiful bar mitzvah in-- in the Warsaw synagogue. Professor Shur was officiated. Koussevitzky was my-- my-- prepared me for the bar mitzvah. Moshe Koussevitzky. And then-- and had my bar mitzvah. Then in 1937, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Avram Stern the founder of the Stern Group, came on behalf of the Irgun to Poland to organize the first school for officers of the-- or instructors, actually, for the Irgun.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he know me. He came. And it was in [POLISH] with the help of the Polish army, which was-- took some [POLISH] this-- negotiating with the Polish government to help to give us military instructors. And Avram Stern was staying in my house without my parents knowing for what purpose. Who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who he was. And I knew Stern before he went to Israel, to Palestine at that time. And I was telling only my parents, it's a friend of mine who came from-- from Palestine here on Betar business. He will stay a few months with us. Since I had the room and participated in this, and I wanted to learn Hebrew. Avram Stern was a poet. He wrote beautiful poetry. I even have some of his poetry. Like the-- 'The Unknown Soldiers,' [SPEAKING HEBREW], which is fire. It's beautiful. And I started to speak to him Hebrew. I didn't know what I am saying, but I was talking. Yeah. Stern didn't understand. I didn't. But I was learning. Finally he got disgusted with me. He started to me, Joseph, tell me what you want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tell him. Said, you don't say it this way, but you say it this way. This. Started to correct me because he was annoyed. Not that he so much, I would say, he wanted to help me, but he was annoyed. He wanted to know what I am saying. And that's how I learned a little bit Hebrew. And I'm good-- not bad at languages. I picked it up. But as a child, before your bar mitzvah preparations, you were not exposed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jewish education.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you were finished with the private public school, where did you continue your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't a private public school. That was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the private elementary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gymnasium, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Private gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to gymnasium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The high-- that was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it private too?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the same school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. It went all the way--

speaker: interviewee
text: I started from first grade to the eighth, and I graduate over there. I got my matura over [INAUDIBLE]. Then I joined and became student of-- a law student at Moshe University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get accepted because of your father's connections?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was the easiest department to be accepted for a Jew. Everything else, it-- if there are numerous clauses or numerous [INAUDIBLE]. We had only a quarter. So this, I-- I-- I was accepted on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you studied law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I studied law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived with your family all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because we couldn't live in the Jewish academic house, only because we were-- I was from Warsaw. That was only for out of-- for out of-- of the city. And they were-- because I-- I was jealous of them, that they live on their-- and I could only-- I spent most of my free time over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to school? Did you have a bicycle, or was it within walking distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too far. I could take us a street car or even walk a little bit. It was about, I would say, 20 minutes walk. Or the street car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there movie theaters in-- in Warsaw? Did-- did youngsters go out? Did you date at that point already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely. When I was a student? Of course, I was already a lot. Of course, I had my friends from Betar, both-- of both sexes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mostly the Jewish crowds stuck together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was always-- since the age of 14, all my friends out of school were Jewish. Of course, I was already in Betar. That's when I joined, in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And there was cultural life, obviously, in-- in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- to want to understand Jews in Warsaw, or Jewish Warsaw, the way we called it, one had to live there. You wouldn't understand. It was a community-- I would say, a country by itself, Jewish Warsaw. Especially Jewish neighborhood. To go out Friday afternoon, look at the kids. Freshly bathed. Dressed for Shabbat. It was really a pleasure to look at. [SPEAKING HEBREW], they say in-- in Hebrew. To look at these kids in preparation of Shabbat. Stores closed. It was-- first of all, Jewish youth, you can't compare American Jewish youth, or American-- or Jewish youth in any country, except maybe Israel. The Jewish youth is organized. Every Jewish kid belong to some kind of a Jewish youth organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it left or right or center? But they belong. Every Friday night, we could go in. I remember a street in the strictly Jewish neighborhood, [INAUDIBLE], and the building was 14. You found all organizations at one building.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Borhov youth, the party Zion, the left, the right party Zion, the Betar, the Hashomer Hatzair. Every in that building, we were going to each other to interrupt. It was we had what to do with ourselves. We were not engaged in a hoo ha. We were-- they were really had a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish theater plays--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and libraries, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at the-- the famous Jewish theater, the [HEBREW] Theater or [HEBREW] Theater. Then it was the [HEBREW]. Then it was a [HEBREW] theater. There were plenty of Jewish-- of Jewish entertainment. Jewish restaurants. I remember we're going at-- sometimes Saturday for a treat with my father to Gatner's restaurant to have [INAUDIBLE]. It was on [INAUDIBLE] right by the synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's where I first I made [INAUDIBLE], was at Gatner's restaurant. Then I had my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life in Warsaw was something impossible to grasp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rich.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a rich life. And first of all, how beautifully they were organized. The social welfare. We had-- you name it. For every purpose, we had an organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Just there. One of the best hospital, which later was taken away by the city because they envied us, that we have one of the best school for nurses, and the best doctors. Doctors who got a world-- were world-renowned. That Dr. [POLISH] for children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then besides that, we had first of all the institutions. The voluntary institutions. The welfare institutions, we would call it now. [SPEAKING HEBREW]. Every district, every neighborhood. Even mine, which was a non-Jewish one, had a [SPEAKING HEBREW]. And [INAUDIBLE] as a welfare-- a-- loans to give, a-- this [HEBREW], which was to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Marry off.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to marry off and to make a-- make endowments for the-- for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Poor brides.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for poor brides. And this [SPEAKING HEBREW], which on Warsaw, the-- in-- on some holidays, Jewish holidays, you allowed to bury, like Rosh Hashanah, [HEBREW], Shabbat, and Yom-- and Yom Kippur. But you couldn't wheel them or drive them. So people who were-- volunteered to carry them from the house to the cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You name it. We had the biggest-- this orphanage house, [POLISH] orphanage house. On [POLISH] 92. I visited them quite often. I visited [POLISH] when he was taken to the [GERMAN]. So he was carrying the-- the children-- two children in his arms. We had synagogues. All kind of synagogues. Ultra orthodox, orthodox synagogues, more conservative. Not conservative in our sense of now, but modern orthodox. We had the Professor Balaban and the [INAUDIBLE], and the [INAUDIBLE] where my sister was married, and my brother was married there. And Professor Balaban, one of the best well-known Jewish historians was the-- the chief rabbi of it. And [POLISH] school had Professional Shur, who was a professor at the Warsaw University of the theological-- this-- department.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you feel that all of this was starting to crack? That somehow things were not running smoothly anymore, and that the order was changing? When did you personally feel that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't crack. We didn't feel it's cracking. But we felt the anti-Semitism on the government level.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Pilsudski's death. After 1935. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That something is cracking. Did we have [POLISH] with his [POLISH] yes. Economic breakout, yes. But hooliganism, no. It came-- that is that today they wanted to start to put, this is a Polish store.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started in 19-- late '30s, '37, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's business affected by it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one episode. What-- when they started to put 'Polish Store,' my father put 'Polish Store.' And they ask him. He say, I am Pole. I'm a Polish citizen. I cannot put 'It's a German store.' A religion is-- is-- Jewish is a religion. However you slice it. Some say it's a nationality. Like I said I had in my documents, military documents, Polish citizen, nationality, Jews, religion, Moses belief. I Have a triple loyal. But this was already my nationalism started to play. If you want it this way, I'll have it-- you'll have it this way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, he took off the sign, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: You finished at gymnasium. You went to law school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finish the law studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was left-- I went to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish army. Couldn't go to any other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got your basic training?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- the-- for divisional-- for division-- at this school-- school for division of infantry. In the-- for reserve officers in [POLISH]. The town is [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there many Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three to start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among how many recruits?

speaker: interviewee
text: About close to-- close to 300. And the only reason I got in is again my father's influence with the high ranking-- this-- members of Polish parliament and government. That was main-- and I really wanted to go. Thus my father used his influence. There were three Jews admitted. The-- the course in the-- in that school for the officers was usually nine months. Then we went for three months to-- to a regular battalion for training or for practice. My two colleagues were kicked out after eight months, just before the-- the graduation. And other-- and were sent to regular battalion where they had to serve 18 months instead of-- instead of 12. Because they found all kind of excuses. I graduated. It wasn't so easy to get through that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't physically--

speaker: interviewee
text: I suffered quite a-- quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emotionally or physically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emotionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Physically, I could take everything. Emotionally was much, much harder. They let me know on every step of the way that I'm a Jew, a stranger from them. You cannot be a professional officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why am I-- so I said, who cares? I have soup at home. Because we call them-- they're going for soup, they become professional-- they're soldiers for the soup. I say, I have soup at home. And with many things, especially on Sundays we felt it the most.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sunday morning, they lined up the battalion. The school was called a battalion. And they lined it up to go to church. And the corporal who hardly knew how to write his own name. Didn't know even how to speak Polish. Was-- used to-- to play a game of words said of [SPEAKING POLISH], Jewish believe. He said, [POLISH] lover, step out. So I didn't budge. My two friends stepped out. They know it meant them. I didn't. So it was all kind of this. Next day, I was taken to a battalion, this report, and I was saying the report was not to me and the order was not given to me. I am a-- a corporal with the-- this, for the census. And not a-- was not a [POLISH] lover. I am [SPEAKING POLISH]. And I didn't take.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, they bowled me out a little bit. But the-- the hell the corporal got, on-- or the-- the platoon-- platoon leader. There was so many occasions like that. And I-- with that-- my stubbornness, I am the Jew. And nothing-- and-- and no one will bother-- nothing will bother me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gained respect. But such a respect, not a presentation from my division could go without me. They always included me. So I became the beloved Jew. Like this. They used to say, every-- every Pole has his [POLISH]. So I become-- may-- maybe I became their [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were graduated from the basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my commanding officer of school said to me I got of-- out of-- out of 300, I had-- you say-- a round of graduates, they got it-- it's a different position in it. It's not the same when it's-- when he is listed. So I was the twelfth listed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see? So my commanding officer said to me, you know, you have a right to be dissatisfied with it. But you understand. It was I couldn't put a Jew higher. So I think I was very happy with-- with twelfth location.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you went back after your-- after the training? You went back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was sent to the city of [POLISH], which is hundred-- close to-- very close to the German border. It was a lot of this-- of Volksdeutsche there, living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ethnic Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ethnic German. And-- but that was before the war. It was 1938. And I was there in basic training till three months. Then I came home a few months later, and I was graduated as a platoon leader-- a platoon leader, actually. And after that, I came home. Started to practice at-- finish?
